section: Evaluation of the density of steels

context_update.summary: Current chunk covers the paper’s abstract, introduction, and a literature review section ('Former studies'). The main objective is density prediction for steels via regression analysis, with two model types: for ferritic steels, one assuming all C as cementite and other solutes insoluble, and another using thermodynamic calculation of cementite amount and solubilities including non-linear Cr, Mn effects. For Ti-stabilized austenitic steels, TiC amount and solubilities are thermodynamically assessed. Key regression findings: Cu, Mo increase and C, Cr, Mn, S, Si, V decrease density in ferritic grades; TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti decrease while Cu, Co, Mo, Ni increase density in austenitic grades. The Former studies section compiles literature on solute effects in bcc/fcc iron: Mn reduces ρ linearly; Ti reduces ρ; Be, Si decrease a while Al, Ti, V, Ge, Mo increase a; Ni increases a up to ~15% then reduces; P decreases a linearly; up to 10% solute, Co has no effect on ρ, Cr decreases ρ, Re, Ir, Pt, Ru, Rh increase ρ. Si up to 5% linearly decreases ρ, and cooling rate matters above 5%. For low-alloy steels, composition changes dominate over microstructure. Cementite density was computed from lattice data, and C effect on martensite density was studied. Table 1 summarizes earlier works.
processing_route: cooling rate after annealing affects density for Si > 5% in bcc iron
key_variables: composition (mass fractions of C, Cr, Mn, Si, Cu, Mo, V, Ni, Ti, N, Co, Al, P, Be, Ge, Re, Ir, Pt, Ru, Rh)
phase constitution (ferrite, austenite, cementite, TiC, martensite)
mechanisms: solid solution effects on lattice parameter and density
composition dominates over microstructure in influencing density for heat-resistant low alloy steels
non-linear effect of Cr and Mn in cementite

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the 'Former studies' section by mentioning oxide effects in sintered steels, cementite density from lattice parameters, and the C effect on martensite density. The 'Data collection' section states that only literature data with exact sample compositions were usable because standard-composition data lack precision; Richter's maximum experimental error is ±0.01%. The 'Data analysis' general part introduces the regression formula 1/ρ_x = Σ r_i C_i^wt(x) with r_i ≈ 1/ρ_i for substitutional solutes. For ferritic steels, two models are described: Model I assumes all C as cementite with zero solubility of other elements, using x = steel, yielding a theoretical r_C = 1.750·10^{-4} m^3/kg; Model II uses thermodynamic calculations to determine cementite amount and solubilities, incorporating non-linear Cr and Mn effects on cementite density, and regresses on ferrite density. For austenitic steels, room-temperature stable fcc requires sufficient Ni, C, N, Mn; γ-Fe density must be extrapolated; Bendick et al. noted contradicting lattice parameter estimates and anomalous thermal expansion. The investigated austenitic steels are single-phase or austenite+TiC; TiC content and solubilities are computed thermodynamically, and regression includes TiC mass fraction. Tables 1-3 provide extensive composition–density correlation data with regression coefficients, apparent densities, deviations, and uncertainties. Subsequent parts will need the regression results and model comparisons to derive predictive equations and discuss elemental effects.
key_variables: mass% C, Cr, Mn, Si, Cu, Mo, V, Ni, Ti, N, Co, Al, P, S, Nb, W
non-linear Cr and Mn influence on cementite density
mechanisms: Regression via 1/ρ form uses r_i ≈ 1/ρ_i for substitutional solutes
C effect modeled via cementite volume fraction (Model I) or separate cementite density (Model II)
Non-linear Cr, Mn effects on cementite density accounted in Model II
TiC formation quantified thermodynamically and included in austenite regression
Anomalous thermal expansion complicates γ-Fe density estimation
figures_or_tables: Table 1: General information for ferritic and low alloy steels (element ranges, densities, literature apparent densities)
Table 2: Regression results for ferritic/low alloy steels Model I (zero solubility in cementite)
Table 3: Regression results for ferrite density Model II (cementite density computed separately)
Table 4: mentioned for γ-Fe lattice parameter (not shown in current chunk)
unresolved_terms: details of oxide effect mechanism on density
specific numerical values of Bendick et al. lattice parameter estimates

context_update.summary: This chunk presents regression results for density of ferritic/low alloy steels (44 batches) using Model I (R²=0.987, Mn outlier 6σ) and Model II (R²=0.994). Both models show Cu, Mo increase density while C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, V decrease it; Al, Nb, P, Ti are insignificant. Model I demonstrates that theoretical 1/ρ_i can substitute missing coefficients. Model II provides cementite density equations: Eq. 6 for Cr (threshold), Eq. 7 for Mn. Austenitic steels (64 batches) are regressed including TiC mass fraction, with TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti reducing ρ_austenite, and Co, Cu, Mo, Ni increasing it (R²=0.975). Apparent density comparisons with elemental and literature values are reported. Key tables (1-4) and figures (1-3) are cited.
key_variables: composition (mass% C, Cr, Mn, Si, Cu, Mo, V, Ni, Ti, N, Co, Al, Nb, P, S)
mechanisms: linear regression of 1/ρ on weight fractions
cementite density linear functions of Cr and Mn
theoretical 1/ρ_i usable for missing substitutional solutes
outlier detection (Mn > 6σ)
thermodynamic cementite calculation improves model accuracy
unresolved_terms: the text associates the threshold for Eq. 6 with C_Mn instead of C_Cr, likely a typographical error

context_update.summary: This chunk discusses the regression results and mechanisms for density of steels. For substitutional solutes, apparent densities are close to elemental densities because they do not strongly affect iron lattice, while covalent-bonding elements strongly affect volume. Figure 2 shows cementite density (Pnma and Pbnm) as a function of Cr and Mn content. Table 4 provides regression coefficients, apparent densities, and comparisons with elemental densities and literature values for austenitic steels. Key findings include: Si in austenite forms covalent bonds and exists in multiple phases, explaining large deviations; Mn in ferrite shows anomalous non-linear effect corrected by Model II by separating cementite contribution; V deviation in Model II attributed to low concentration (0.08 %) and non-equilibrium; 5–8% density increase for fcc-Fe vs bcc from Zwell et al. explains Ni's lower apparent density in bcc-iron. Table 4 contains extensive data for TiC, C, Co, Cr, Cu, Mn, Mo, N, Ni, Si, Ti, γ-Fe with regression coefficients, apparent densities, deviations and uncertainties. Future parts may use these coefficients for density prediction and model validation.
mechanisms: Substitutional solutes do not strongly affect iron lattice; covalent bonding elements significantly affect volume
Mn in ferrite density corrected by separate cementite calculation (Model II)
Low V content causes slow diffusion and likely non-equilibrium, undermining Model II assumption
Si forms covalent bonds in austenite and exists in multiple phases, causing large density deviations
Coordination number changes affect atomic diameter; 5-8% density increase for fcc-Fe vs bcc explains Ni lower apparent density in bcc-iron

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the Discussion and Conclusions. It specifies that regression coefficients are valid within the single-phase concentration range or the two-phase region when the phase boundary is well below 0.01 mass%. The main conclusions from the regression analysis are presented: in ferritic steels density increases with Cu and Mo and decreases with C (cementite), Cr, Mn, S, Si, and V; in austenitic steels density decreases with C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti, and TiC and increases with Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni. Practical guidelines are given: Equation (4) with coefficients from Table 2 (ferritic) or Table 4 (austenitic) can estimate density; improved accuracy for ferritic and Ti-stabilized austenitic steels requires thermodynamic analysis using Equations (6, 7) and Table 3; and if no regression data exist, literature values or coefficients from pure element densities can be used. No new figures or tables appear; previously introduced Tables 2, 3, and 4 are referenced. Future parts may apply these predictive rules and coefficients for density calculations and model validation.
mechanisms: regression analysis of composition-density relationship
separate treatment of cementite and TiC effects

Table 4 contains extensive data for TiC, C, Co, Cr, Cu, Mn, Mo, N, Ni, Si, Ti, γ-Fe with regression coefficients, apparent densities, deviations and uncertainties. Future parts may use these coefficients for density prediction and model validation. This chunk covers the Discussion and Conclusions. It specifies that regression coefficients are valid within the single-phase concentration range or the two-phase region when the phase boundary is well below 0.01 mass%. The main conclusions from the regression analysis are presented: in ferritic steels density increases with Cu and Mo and decreases with C (cementite), Cr, Mn, S, Si, and V; in austenitic steels density decreases with C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti, and TiC and increases with Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni. Practical guidelines are given: Equation (4) with coefficients from Table 2 (ferritic) or Table 4 (austenitic) can estimate density; improved accuracy for ferritic and Ti-stabilized austenitic steels requires thermodynamic analysis using Equations (6, 7) and Table 3; and if no regression data exist, literature values or coefficients from pure element densities can be used. No new figures or tables appear; previously introduced Tables 2, 3, and 4 are referenced. Future parts may apply these predictive rules and coefficients for density calculations and model validation.
Material systems: ferritic and low alloy steels; austenitic steels; Ti-stabilized austenitic steels; bcc iron; fcc iron; cementite (Fe3C); TiC; martensite
Processing route: cooling rate after annealing affects density for Si > 5% in bcc iron
Key variables: composition (mass fractions of C, Cr, Mn, Si, Cu, Mo, V, Ni, Ti, N, Co, Al, P, Be, Ge, Re, Ir, Pt, Ru, Rh); lattice parameter a; density ρ; temperature; cooling rate; phase constitution (ferrite, austenite, cementite, TiC, martensite); cementite amount; TiC amount
Microstructure: ferrite; austenite; cementite; TiC; martensite; bcc iron; fcc iron; TiC precipitates
Properties: density; lattice parameter; elastic modulus; density ρ (kg/m³ or g/cm³); regression coefficients r_i; apparent density; experimental error ±0.01%; density ρ_steel
Mechanisms: solid solution effects on lattice parameter and density; composition dominates over microstructure in influencing density for heat-resistant low alloy steels; non-linear effect of Cr and Mn in cementite; Regression via 1/ρ form uses r_i ≈ 1/ρ_i for substitutional solutes; C effect modeled via cementite volume fraction (Model I) or separate cementite density (Model II); Non-linear Cr, Mn effects on cementite density accounted in Model II; TiC formation quantified thermodynamically and included in austenite regression; Anomalous thermal expansion complicates γ-Fe density estimation
